elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, the method of gaps, a technique for deriving closed-form expressions in terms of information measures for the generalization error of machine learning algorithms is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-002: The method relies on two central observations: $(a)$~The generalization error is an average of the variation of the expected empirical risk with respect to changes on the probability measure (used for expectation); and~$(b)$~these variations, also referred to as gaps, exhibit closed-form expressions in terms of information measures.
elements.sent-2411-003: The expectation of the empirical risk can be either with respect to a measure on the models (with a fixed dataset) or with respect to a measure on the datasets (with a fixed model), which results in two variants of the method of gaps.
elements.sent-2411-004: The first variant, which focuses on the gaps of the expected empirical risk with respect to a measure on the models, appears to be the most general, as no assumptions are made on the distribution of the datasets.
elements.sent-2411-005: The second variant develops under the assumption that datasets are made of independent and identically distributed data points.
elements.sent-2411-006: All existing exact expressions for the generalization error of machine learning algorithms can be obtained with the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-007: Also, this method allows obtaining numerous new exact expressions, which improves the understanding of the generalization error; establish connections with other areas in statistics, e.g., hypothesis testing; and potentially, might guide algorithm designs.
elements.sent-2411-008: The generalization error in statistical machine learning is the expectation (w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-009: The generalization error has become a key metric for assessing an algorithm’s performance and its ability to generalize beyond training data.
elements.sent-2411-010: Traditional approaches for analyzing the generalization error generally fall into two categories.
elements.sent-2411-011: The first category derives upper bounds on the generalization error based on properties of the hypothesis class, such as Rademacher complexity and VC dimension \cite{vapnik2006estimation, alon1997scale, mohri2008rademacher, shalev2014understanding, cherkassky1999model .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The second category examines the dependency between learning algorithms and data distributions through various information-theoretic metrics \cite{jiao2017dependence, wang2019information, issa2019strengthened, asadi2018chaining, lopez2018generalization, asadi2020chaining, hafez2020conditioning, haghifam2020sharpened, rodriguez2021tighter .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The initial exact characterizations of generalization error using information measures were introduced for the Gibbs algorithm in \cite{aminian2021exact and further expanded in \cite{aminian2024information .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: A more general result, which considers a $\sigma$-finite measure as the reference for the Gibbs algorithm, was presented in \cite{PerlazaTIT2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: For learning algorithms beyond Gibbs, two main types of guarantees are provided under the information-theoretic framework.
elements.sent-2411-016: The first type is often referred to as guarantees in probability, which assert that with high probability over the probability measure of the datasets, the expected risk induced by the learning algorithm is bounded above by a certain threshold.
elements.sent-2411-017: This type of guarantees is referred to as PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds as in \cite{catoni2007pac, viallard2024learning, AlquierPACjournal2024,shawe1997pac, guedj2019free, mcallester2003pac, haddouche2020pacbayes .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In parallel, guarantees in expectation provide bounds on the generalization error.
elements.sent-2411-019: To account for the dependence between the learning algorithm and the data distribution, mutual information is a common metric in these analyses.
elements.sent-2411-020: Such information measure was first introduced in \cite{pmlr-v51-russo16 and later extended in \cite{xu2017information .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Chaining techniques were incorporated in \cite{asadi2018chaining, asadi2020chaining to derive upper bounds, with similar bounds appearing in \cite{bu2020tightening, rodriguez2021upper and related studies.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Alternative metrics such as Wasserstein distance \cite{aminian2022tighter, lopez2018generalization, wang2019information, rodriguez2021tighter , maximal leakage \cite{issa2019strengthened, esposito2020robust , mutual $f$-information \cite{masiha2023f , and Jensen-Shannon divergence \cite{aminian2021jensen have also been used to derive generalization bounds.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Comprehensive literature reviews of these guarantees can be found in \cite{hellstrom2023generalization and, more recently, in \cite{rodriguez2024information .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Nonetheless, for any specific use, the tightness of many of these bounds cannot be guaranteed, as noted in~\cite{Jiang2020Fantastic and \cite{gastpar2023fantastic .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Recently, exact characterizations of the generalization error, applicable to any statistical learning algorithm, were provided in \cite{zouJSAIT2024 and \cite{InriaRR9539 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: These new expressions involve several information measures, e.g., relative entropy, mutual information and lautum information \cite{palomar2008lautum .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The present work is dedicated to deriving closed-form expressions for the generalization error of machine learning algorithms through the lens of information theory.
elements.sent-2411-028: The main contribution is a general method for such a purpose, which has been coined the method of gaps.
elements.sent-2411-029: Interestingly, all existing exact expressions for the generalization error of machine learning algorithms can be obtained with the proposed method.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Gaps and the Method of Gaps
elements.sent-2411-030: In order to introduce the method of gaps, first the notion of gap is defined.
elements.sent-2411-031: A gap is the difference between two expectations of the empirical risk, each calculated using a different probability measure, both on the same measurable space.
elements.sent-2411-032: Hence, a gap can be understood as a measure of the variation of the expected empirical risk due to a change on the measure used to take such an expectation.
elements.sent-2411-033: The expectation of the empirical risk can be taken with respect to either $(1)$ a probability measure over the set of models, keeping the dataset fixed; or $(2)$ a probability measure over the set of datasets, keeping the model fixed.
elements.sent-2411-034: Variations in the first case are often called algorithm-driven gaps.
elements.sent-2411-035: This is because probability measures on the set of models correspond to different machine learning algorithms, meaning these variations reflect changes in the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-036: Variations in the second case are known as data-driven gaps.
elements.sent-2411-037: These arise from changes in the probability measures over the datasets, which can be interpreted as changes in the statistical properties of the data.
elements.sent-2411-038: Certain algorithm-driven and data-driven gaps have been shown to have closed-form expressions in terms of relative entropies.
elements.sent-2411-039: The first closed-form expressions of an algorithm-driven gap were presented in \cite[Theorem~$1$]{Perlaza-ISIT2023b to quantify the variation of the expected empirical risk due to a deviation from the Gibbs algorithm to an alternative algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-040: This foundational result serves as a key building block in this work for deriving closed-form expressions for algorithm-driven gaps due to transitions between arbitrary algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-041: See for instance, Theorem~\ref{TheoremRGgeneral in Section~\ref{SecSensitivityArbitraryA .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The first-closed form expression for a data-driven gap due to a change from a worst-case data-generating (WCDG) probability measure to an arbitrary measure was presented in \cite[Theorem~$5.2$]{zou2024Generalization .}
elements.sent-2411-043: This crucial result formed the basis for deriving the closed-form expressions for data-driven gaps due to a changes from and to arbitrary probability measures in \cite[Theorem~$8$]{zouJSAIT2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: In a nutshell, exact expressions for algorithm-driven gaps involve the Gibbs algorithm studied in~\cite{PerlazaTIT2024 , while exact expressions for data-driven gaps involve the WCDG probability measure introduced in~\cite{zou2024Generalization .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: In both cases, these expressions are surprisingly simple and flexible, as they contain free parameters that can be chosen subject to few constraints.
elements.sent-2411-046: From this perspective, two variants of the method of gaps can be identified.
elements.sent-2411-047: The former, which is based on algorithm-driven gaps, involves two steps:~$(i)$ To express the generalization error as the expectation (with respect to a probability measure on the datasets) of a particular algorithm-driven gap (see Lemma~\ref{LemmaJun4at9h32in2024 in Section~\ref{SecCharAlgorithmDrivenGaps ); and~$(ii)$ To exploit the closed-form expressions for algorithm-driven gaps presented in Section~\ref{SecSensitivityArbitraryA to obtain expressions for the generalization error.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The method of gaps via algorithm-driven gaps, was implicitly used to obtain the celebrated exact expression of the generalization error of the Gibbs algorithm in \cite[Theorem~$17$]{PerlazaTIT2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Albeit no particular mention to a method is made therein, \cite[Theorem~$16$]{PerlazaTIT2024 can be associated to step one of the method of gaps; and \cite[Theorem~$17$]{PerlazaTIT2024 to step two.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Unfortunately, the closed-form expressions for algorithm-driven gaps developed in \cite{Perlaza-ISIT-2022, PerlazaTIT2024 are limited to transitions from the Gibbs algorithm to alternative algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-051: This limits such a theoretical framework to obtain exact expressions for the generalization error of arbitrary algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-052: Nonetheless, these findings are essential for obtaining the results presented in this work.
elements.sent-2411-053: The second variant of the method of gaps, which is based on data-driven gaps consists also in two steps:~$(i)$ To express the generalization error as the expectation (with respect to a probability measure on the models) of a particular data-driven gap (see \cite[Equation~$(75)$]{zouJSAIT2024 ); and~$(ii)$ To exploit the closed-form expressions for data-driven gaps presented in Section~\ref{SecDataDrivenGaps to obtain expressions for the generalization error.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: This second variant was the method of proof used in~\cite{zouJSAIT2024 to obtain the first exact expression \cite[Theorem~$10$]{zouJSAIT2024 for the generalization error of arbitrary machine learning algorithms.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Using the same method of proof, other closed-form expressions \cite[Theorem~$11$ and Theorem~$13$]{InriaRR9539 have been obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The expressions obtained via this second variant of the method of gaps are surprisingly flexible, as they depend on parameters that can be arbitrarily chosen subject to mild conditions.
elements.sent-2411-057: However, these expressions are developed under the assumption that datasets consist of data points that are independent and identically distributed.
elements.sent-2411-058: Interestingly, this assumption can be relaxed to identically distributed datapoints, but such a relaxation has not been explored in this work.
elements.sent-2411-059: The main contribution of this work consists in a novel method for developing closed-form expressions for the generalization error of machine learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-060: The technique features two distinct variants: One based on algorithm-driven gaps and another one based on data-driven gaps.
elements.sent-2411-061: While the variant based on data-driven gaps is established, having been used in \cite{zou2024Generalization,zouJSAIT2024 and \cite{InriaRR9539 , the variant based on algorithm-driven gaps is original and yields innovative expressions for the generalization error.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: More importantly, this variant does not require the assumption that datasets are made of independent and identically distributed data points, which is the underlying assumption in the variant based on data-driven gaps.
elements.sent-2411-063: Each variant of the method of gaps leads to different equivalent expressions for the generalization error of machine learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-064: Nonetheless, a common structure consisting of the addition of two terms can be identified.
elements.sent-2411-065: Independently of the variant, the first term, up to a constant factor, is either the mutual information or the lautum information between the models and the datasets.
elements.sent-2411-066: The second term is particular for each variant.
elements.sent-2411-067: Nonetheless, such terms appear to highlight some ``geometric'' duality between the two variants.
elements.sent-2411-068: From this perspective, another key contribution of this work is the establishment of parallels between the two variants of the method of gaps using simple connections to existing problems, for instance, mismatched hypothesis testing~\cite{boroumand2022mismatched .}
elements.sent-2411-069: While these arguments illuminate interesting findings, many intriguing aspects of the expressions obtained with the method of gaps are left unexplored and open for future research.

elements.sent-2411-467: In this work, closed-form expressions for the generalization error have been derived in terms of information-theoretic measures.
elements.sent-2411-468: Two distinct classes of expressions are identified.
elements.sent-2411-469: The first class encompasses formulations involving the Gibbs algorithm, as defined by the Gibbs probability measure in Definition~\ref{DefGibbsAlgorithm .}
elements.sent-2411-470: The second class pertains to expressions incorporating the WCDG probability measure, defined in Definition~\ref{DefWCDG .}
elements.sent-2411-471: While the former represents a more general framework, as it imposes no specific conditions on the probability distribution of datasets, both classes offer unique and complementary insights.
elements.sent-2411-472: These insights establish connections between the generalization error and concepts such as hypothesis testing, information measures, and Euclidean geometry.
elements.sent-2411-473: However, these connections have been only briefly explored in this work, and their full potential to enhance the understanding of generalization error remains an open avenue for further research.
elements.sent-2411-474: It is important to note that the derived expressions do not significantly simplify the numerical computation of the generalization error.
elements.sent-2411-475: This is unsurprising, as the primary goal of this study is to provide a deeper conceptual understanding of the generalization error from an information-theoretic perspective.
elements.sent-2411-476: Specifically, the established connections between the generalization error, the Gibbs algorithm, and the WCDG probability measure represent a significant advancement in this goal.
elements.sent-2411-477: Concerning the connection to the Gibbs algorithm, probably, the expression that best unveils the relevance of these findings is the one in Theorem~\ref{TheoremJuin26at11h03in2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-478: Note that such expression implies that the generalization error of an arbitrary machine learning algorithm can be understood as a comparison, via relative entropy, of all possible instances of the algorithm under study with all possible instances of the Gibbs algorithm in two scenarios: $(a)$ Both algorithms are trained upon the same training datasets drawn from the same probability distribution; and $(b)$ Both algorithms are trained upon training datasets independently drawn from the same probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-479: Alternatively, concerning the connection to the WCDG probability measure, probably, the expression that best unveils the relevance of these findings is the one in Theorem~\ref{TheoremTrainEntropyGE .}
elements.sent-2411-480: The expression therein implies that the generalization error of a given machine learning algorithm consists of a comparison, via relative entropy, of all possible likelihoods on the datapoints induced by the algorithm under study; and the WCDG probability measure in two scenarios: $(a)$ both probability measures, e.g., likelihood and WDCG probability measures, are conditioned on the same model drawn from the same probability distribution; and $(b)$ both probability measures are respectively conditioned upon models independently drawn from the same probability distribution.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran \appendices.}
